event: Paleo-Indian peoples settle the region, leaving evidence at sites such as Pedra Furada and Lagoa Santa.
The skeletal remains later named 'Luzia,' among the oldest in the Americas, are deposited near present-day Lagoa Santa.
Coastal peoples build extensive sambaqui shell mounds along the Atlantic seaboard.
Marajoara culture begins developing complex pottery and earthworks on Marajó Island in the Amazon estuary.
Tupi-Guarani peoples expand across much of the coast and interior, becoming the dominant indigenous groups at European contact.
Portuguese navigator Pedro Álvares Cabral lands on the Brazilian coast and claims the territory for Portugal.
Martim Afonso de Sousa founds São Vicente, the first permanent Portuguese settlement in Brazil.
Portugal divides the colony into hereditary captaincies to spur settlement and defense.
Tomé de Sousa becomes the first governor-general and founds Salvador as the colonial capital.
Rio de Janeiro is founded after Portuguese forces expel French colonists from the bay.
The Dutch West India Company captures Pernambuco, establishing Dutch Brazil centered on Recife.
Portuguese-Brazilian forces expel the Dutch, ending Dutch control of the northeast.
The maroon republic of Palmares is destroyed by colonial forces after decades of resistance led by Zumbi.
Diamonds are discovered in Minas Gerais, fueling a mining boom that reshapes the colonial economy.
The colonial capital is moved from Salvador to Rio de Janeiro amid the gold-mining boom in the interior.
The Inconfidência Mineira, an independence conspiracy, is suppressed and its leader Tiradentes is later executed.
The Portuguese royal court flees Napoleon and relocates to Rio de Janeiro, opening Brazil's ports to world trade.
Brazil is elevated to a kingdom united with Portugal and the Algarves.
Prince Pedro declares independence with the 'Cry of Ipiranga' and becomes Emperor Pedro I of Brazil.
Brazil adopts its first constitution, establishing a centralized constitutional monarchy.
Pedro I abdicates in favor of his young son Pedro II, beginning a regency period.
Pedro II is declared of age and assumes the throne, inaugurating a long period of relative stability.
Brazil joins Argentina and Uruguay in the War of the Triple Alliance against Paraguay.
The Lei Áurea abolishes slavery, freeing the last enslaved people in the Americas.
A military coup overthrows the monarchy and proclaims the Republic of Brazil.
Brazil adopts a republican federal constitution modeled on the United States.
Government forces crush the Canudos rebellion in the impoverished backlands of Bahia.
Brazil declares war on Germany and joins the Allies in World War I.
A revolution brings Getúlio Vargas to power, ending the Old Republic's coffee-oligarchy politics.
Vargas establishes the authoritarian Estado Novo regime with a new corporatist constitution.
Brazil declares war on the Axis powers and later sends an expeditionary force to fight in Italy.
Vargas is deposed and Brazil becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Brazil wins its first FIFA World Cup in Sweden, with a teenage Pelé emerging as a global star.
The new capital, Brasília, designed by Oscar Niemeyer and Lúcio Costa, is inaugurated under President Juscelino Kubitschek.
A military coup overthrows President João Goulart, beginning a 21-year military dictatorship.
Institutional Act No. 5 intensifies repression, censorship, and torture during the dictatorship's hardest years.
Brazil wins its third World Cup in Mexico, permanently retaining the Jules Rimet Trophy.
The military hands power to a civilian government, restoring democracy after Tancredo Neves's election.
A new democratic constitution, the 'Citizen Constitution,' is promulgated.
Rio de Janeiro hosts the Earth Summit, and President Fernando Collor resigns amid impeachment for corruption.
The Plano Real introduces a new currency and tames hyperinflation, and Brazil wins its fourth World Cup.
Brazil wins a record fifth World Cup, and Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva is elected president.
Dilma Rousseff is elected as Brazil's first female president.
Brazil hosts the FIFA World Cup amid mass protests, as the Lava Jato corruption investigation unfolds.
President Dilma Rousseff is impeached and removed from office, and Rio de Janeiro hosts the Summer Olympics.
Jair Bolsonaro is elected president, marking a sharp shift to the far right.
Lula da Silva is elected to a third term, narrowly defeating Bolsonaro.
Supporters of Bolsonaro storm government buildings in Brasília in an attempted insurrection days after Lula's inauguration.
